attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a five-day history of breathlessness, reduced exercise tolerance, fatigue and malaise. She has been coughing up blood-streaked sputum. She has not noticed an increase in her usual sputum volume. She has a medical history of bronchiectasis diagnosed eight years ago. She takes no regular medications. She is a non-smoker and works as a teacher.

On examination, she appears mildly unwell. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Chest examination reveals coarse crackles at both lung bases. A chest X-ray shows chronic bronchiectatic changes with no acute consolidation. Sputum has been sent for microscopy, culture and sensitivity. There are no local antibiotic guidelines available, and no prior sputum culture results are accessible.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with an acute exacerbation of bronchiectasis, characterised by increased breathlessness, reduced exercise tolerance, fatigue, malaise, productive cough and blood-streaked sputum. Even without increased sputum production, antibiotic therapy is indicated for bronchiectasis exacerbations.

Empirical amoxicillin for 14 days is the most appropriate management. Guidelines recommend 14 days of antibiotic therapy for bronchiectasis exacerbations. While antibiotic selection should ideally be guided by local guidelines and prior sputum culture results, neither are available in this case. Sputum culture has already been sent and will help identify the causative organism and guide targeted therapy if needed. Amoxicillin is a reasonable empirical choice covering common respiratory pathogens including Haemophilus influenzae and Streptococcus pneumoniae. Additionally, viral respiratory PCR should be considered as viral infections can trigger exacerbations.

CT thoracic angiogram would be indicated for massive haemoptysis or at CTPA if there was suspected pulmonary embolism. Given the small volume haemoptysis (blood streaks) in the context of a bronchiectasis exacerbation, this investigation is unlikely to identify a specific bleeding source and is not necessary.

Azithromycin for three days represents inadequate treatment duration. While azithromycin is an appropriate antibiotic choice for bronchiectasis exacerbations, the standard treatment duration is 14 days, not three days.

Nebulised tranexamic acid may be considered for significant haemoptysis in bronchiectasis, but is not standard therapy for small volume haemoptysis during an exacerbation. The priority is treating the underlying infection.

Oral prednisolone is not routinely recommended for bronchiectasis exacerbations unless there is concurrent asthma, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, or vasculitis. Corticosteroids may impair host defence mechanisms and are generally avoided in bronchiectasis.

Bronchiectasis is characterised by permanent dilatation of the bronchi with impaired mucociliary clearance, leading to recurrent infections. Exacerbations are typically caused by bacterial pathogens including Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, though viral infections can also be triggers. Without access to prior sputum results or local guidelines, empirical therapy must cover the most common pathogens while awaiting current culture results.
learningPoints: Bronchiectasis exacerbations should be treated with antibiotics for 14 days, even without increased sputum production.
Sputum microscopy, culture and sensitivity should guide antibiotic therapy in bronchiectasis exacerbations.
Small volume haemoptysis in bronchiectasis exacerbation does not require further workup if there are no additional concerning features.
references: Chang AB, Bell SC, Byrnes CA, Dawkins P, Holland AE, Kennedy E, et al. Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) position statement on chronic suppurative lung disease and bronchiectasis in children, adolescents and adults in Australia and New Zealand. Respirol Carlton Vic. 2023
Bronchiectasis. Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; 2025 [cited 2025 Jul 17]. Available from: https://www.tg.org.au
